30: kai tote phanesetai to semeion tou huiou tou anthropou en ourano — and then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in heaven — the sign (semeion) is disputed: whether the cross appearing in the sky, or the Son of Man himself as the sign, or the Shekinah-cloud. The citation of Dan 7:13 that follows (erchomenon epi ton nephelos tou ouranou) suggests the sign is the appearing of the Son of Man himself in the divine glory-cloud. kopsontai pasai hai phylai tes ges — all the tribes of the earth will mourn — citing Zech 12:10 (the mourning that follows seeing the one they pierced); grief and recognition converge at the parousia.
1: eporeueto apo tou hierou — was going away from the temple — Poreuomai (imperfect: was going away) signals a definitive departure from the temple. Prosēlthon hoi mathētai autou epideixai autō̧ tās oikodomās tou hierou (the disciples came to point out the buildings of the temple) — oikodomās (buildings, constructions) in the plural refers to the entire temple complex.
2: ou mē aphethē̄ ōde lithos epi lithon — there will by no means be left here stone upon stone — Ou mē + aorist passive subjunctive = absolute negation of any remaining structure. Hos ou kataly thēsetai (that will not be thrown down) — katalyō (to loosen down, demolish) is used both literally for building-destruction and metaphorically (26:61; 27:40). Fulfilled in AD 70 under Titus.
3: tis hē sēmeia tēs sēs parousias kai synteleiās tou aiōnos — what is the sign of your presence/coming and of the completion of the age? — Parousia (presence, arrival — used for royal/imperial visits) is the technical NT term for the Second Advent. Synteleias tou aiōnos (completion/end of the age) — synteleia is consummation, bringing to full end; tou aiōnos = of the age/era.
4: blepete mē tis hymas planēsē̄ — see to it that no one leads you astray — Planāō (to cause to wander, to mislead) is the eschatological deception verb; it recurs in vv.5, 11, 24. The primary danger is not persecution but deception — being misled about the identity of the Christ and the timing of the end.
5: polloi gar eleusontai epi tō̄ onomati mou legontes egō eimi ho christos — for many will come in my name saying I am the Christ — Epi tō̄ onomati mou (in/on my name) = using the authority of the name of Jesus as their credential. Egō eimi ho christos (I am the Christ) — the false messiah claim. Pollōs planēsousin (they will lead many astray) — the deception’s scope is wide.
6: mellēsete de akouein polemos kai akoai polemōn — you will hear of wars and rumors of wars — Akoai polemōn (reports/rumors of wars) — the hearing of distant conflicts. Horāte mē throeisthe (see that you are not alarmed) — throeō (to cry out in alarm, to be disturbed) — these are conditions not to be interpreted as the end: all’ oupo estin to telos (but the end is not yet).
7: egerthēsetai gar ethnos epi ethnos kai basileia epi basileian — for nation will rise against nation and kingdom against kingdom — The double epi (against) construction with repetition is a LXX war-oracle pattern (Isa 19:2; 2 Chr 15:6). Loimoi kai seismoi kata topous (plagues and earthquakes in various places) — kata topous (in various/diverse places) limits the scope: localized, not final.
8: panta de tauta archē ōdinōn — all these are the beginning of birth pangs — Ōdines (birth pains, labor pangs) is an OT apocalyptic metaphor for the distresses preceding the Messianic age (Isa 26:17; Jer 22:23; Mic 4:9-10; 1QH). Archē (beginning) positions these events as the onset of a process, not the culmination.
9: tote paradosousin hymas eis thlipsin — then they will hand you over into tribulation — Paradidōmi (to hand over, the passion vocabulary) is now applied to disciples. Thlipsis (tribulation, pressure, affliction) — the same word used for the great tribulation in v.21. Apoktenousin hymas (will kill you) — direct persecution. Misounōmenoi hypo pantōn tōn ethnōn (hated by all the nations) — universal hostility.
10: kai tote skandalisthēsontai polloi — and then many will fall away — Skandalizō (to cause to stumble, to cause to fall away) — the passive future: many will be caused to stumble from faith. Allēlous paradosousin (they will betray one another) — communal breakdown; persecution produces internal betrayal. Misēsousin allēlous (will hate one another).
11: polloi pseudoprophetai egerthesontai — many false prophets will arise — Pseudoprophētēs (false prophet) — one who claims prophetic authority falsely. Planēsousin pollous (will lead many astray) — the deception vocabulary recurs (cf. v.4,5). The false prophets operate within the community, not merely from outside.
12: dia to plēthyn thēnai tēn anomian psēgēsetai hē agapē tōn pollōn — because of the increase of lawlessness the love of many will grow cold — Anomīa (lawlessness) as cause; psúchō (to cool down, grow cold) as effect on agapē (love). The causal connection: sustained exposure to ethical collapse erodes love. Tōn pollōn = of the many (the majority), not universally.
13: ho de hypominās eis telos houtos sōthēsetai — but the one who endures to the end will be saved — Hypominō (to remain under, to endure — not passive resignation but active steadfastness under pressure). Eis telos (to the end) — whether the end of one’s life or the end of the age. Sōthēsetai (will be saved) future passive = eschatological salvation.
14: kērych thēsetai touto to euangelion tēs basileias en holȩ̄ tē̄ oikoumenē̄ — this gospel of the kingdom will be proclaimed in all the inhabited world — Oikoumenē (the inhabited world — the Greek term for the civilized/Roman world) is here given universal scope. Eis martyrion pasin tois ethnesin (as a testimony to all the nations) — martyrion (witness, testimony, evidence). Kai tote hēxei to telos (and then the end will come) — the gospel-to-all-nations is a precondition for the end.
15: to bdelygma tēs erēmōseōs — the abomination of desolation — Bdelygma (abomination — from bdelyssōmai = to be disgusted by, to abhor) is the LXX term for idolatrous desecration (Dan 9:27; 11:31; 12:11 LXX). Hestōs en topō̧ hagiō̧ (standing in a holy place) — the masculine participle hestōs with the neuter noun bdelygma may suggest a person (antichrist figure) rather than an object. Ho anaginnōskōn noeitō (let the reader understand) is a direct address to the reader of the written prophecy.
16: tote hoi en tē̄ Ioudaiā̧ pheugetōsan eis ta orē — then let those in Judea flee to the mountains — Pheugetōsan (present imperative 3rd plural: let them flee, keep fleeing) — urgency of continuous flight. The command applies to those in Judea specifically, not universally; the geographical specificity supports a first-century Jerusalem referent.
17: ho epi tou dōmatos mē katabatō — let the one on the rooftop not come down — Palestinian flat-roofed houses had exterior stairs; one could flee directly from rooftop to street without entering the house to gather possessions. Ârai ta ek tēs oikias autou (to take the things from his house) — the urgency of escape outweighs property retrieval.
18: kai ho en tō̄ agrō̧ mē epistrepsatō opisō — and the one in the field, let him not turn back — Epistrepho (to turn back, return) — a worker in the field should not return home for his cloak (himation). The same urgency governs both rooftop and field scenarios.
19: ouai de tais en gastri echousais kai tais thēlazousais — woe to those who are pregnant and to those nursing — Ouai here is genuine lamentation over physical vulnerability in flight: pregnant women and nursing mothers cannot flee quickly. The ouai is compassionate, not judicial.
20: proseuchesthe de hina mē genētai hē phygē hymōn cheimonos mēde sabbatō̧ — pray that your flight not happen in winter nor on a Sabbath — Winter flight is physically dangerous (flooding wadis, cold). Sabbath flight raises a Jewish law issue: travel beyond the Sabbath limit (techum Shabbat = ~2000 cubits) is prohibited; a Sabbath escape would force a choice between legal observance and survival. The prayer assumes continued Sabbath observance in the community.
21: estai gar tote thlipsis megalē hoia ou gegonen ap’ archēs kosmou — for then there will be great tribulation such as has not been from the beginning of the world — Thlipsis megalē (great tribulation) — the decisive eschatological crisis. The comparison formula hoia ou gegonen (such as has not been) echoes Dan 12:1 LXX. The phrase heōs tou nyn (until now) + oud’ ou mē genētai (nor will ever be) brackets the event as uniquely catastrophic.
22: ei mē ekolobōthēsan hai hēmerai ekeinai ouk an esōthē pasa sarx — if those days had not been cut short, no flesh would be saved — Koloboō (to cut short, to mutilate by cutting off — from kolobos = maimed) — the shortening is divine mercy. Pasa sarx (all flesh) = all living humans. Dia de tous eklektous (but for the sake of the elect) — the elect’s preservation motivates the divine shortening of tribulation.
23: tote ean tis hymin eipē̄ idou hōde ho christos ē hōde — then if anyone says to you: here is the Christ, or here! — The repeated warning against false location-claims for the Christ (vv.23-26) uses idou hōde... ē hōde (here... or there) to characterize false messiahs as geographically bounded. Mē pisteusēte (do not believe) — aorist subjunctive prohibition.
24: egerthēsontai gar pseudochristoi kai pseudoprophetai — for false christs and false prophets will arise — Pseudochristos (false christ, counterfeit anointed one) and pseudoprophētēs (false prophet) are paired. Dōsousin sēmeia megala kai terata (will give great signs and wonders) — the deception’s power is real; the signs are genuine miracles. Hōste planēsai ei dynaton kai tous eklektous (so as to lead astray, if possible, even the elect) — ei dynaton (if possible) — the elect cannot ultimately be permanently deceived, but the attempt is real.
25: idou proeireka hymin — behold I have told you beforehand — Prolegō (to say beforehand, to forewarn) perfect active: the warning is completed and stands on record. The pre-announcement serves as authentication when the events occur and as inoculation against surprise.
26: ean oun eipōsin hymin idou en tē̄ erēmō̄ estin mē exelthēte — if therefore they say: he is in the wilderness, do not go out — Two false-location scenarios: wilderness (erēmos — where messianic movements gathered, cf. Acts 21:38) and inner rooms (tameiois — secret, hidden location). Both are rejected: the true Parousia will not be localized or secret.
27: hōsper gar hē astrapē exerchetai apo anatolōn — for as the lightning comes from the east — Astrapē (lightning flash) + phainetai heōs dysmōn (shines to the west) — lightning is universally visible, not localized. The Parousia of the Son of Man will be similarly visible to all simultaneously, requiring no one to point it out. Houtōs estai hē parousia tou huiou tou anthrōpou — the comparison is precise.
28: hopou ean ȩ̄ to ptōma ekei synachthēsontai hoi aetoi — wherever the corpse is there the eagles will be gathered — Ptōma (fallen body, corpse) and aetoi (eagles or vultures; in Palestine, likely griffon vultures that are drawn to carrion from great distances). The proverb may signal that judgment follows its object as inevitably as scavengers find a carcass, or that the Parousia (like lightning v.27) is unmistakably obvious when it comes.
29: eutheos de meta tēn thlipsin tōn hēmerōn ekeinōn — immediately after the tribulation of those days — Eutheōs (immediately, at once) connects the cosmic signs directly to the tribulation. Ho hēlios skotisthēsetai (the sun will be darkened) — cosmic de-creation language from Isa 13:10 (Babylon oracle) and Joel 2:10,31; 3:15. The powers of the heavens being shaken (hai dynameis tōn ouranōn saleuthhsontai) echoes Isa 34:4 and Hag 2:6,21.
31: apostelei tous angelous autou meta sal pignos megalēs phōnēs — he will send his angels with a great trumpet sound — Salpi nx megalē (great trumpet) echoes Isa 27:13 (the great trumpet gathering the scattered of Israel) and the Shofar Gadol of Jewish eschatology. Episynaxousin tous eklektous autou (will gather together his elect) — episynagō (to gather together to oneself) from ek tōn tessarōn anemōn (from the four winds) = universal gathering from every direction.
32: apo de tēs sykēs mathete tēn parabolēn — from the fig tree learn the parable — Mathete (learn, aorist imperative from manthanō) the lesson that nature teaches. Hotan ēdē ho klados autēs genētai hapalos (when already its branch becomes tender) — hapalos (tender, soft) of fresh spring growth. The fig tree’s budding is a natural sign that summer (theros) is near; the eschatological signs similarly indicate nearness.
33: ginos kete hoti eggys estin epi thyrais — know that it is near, at the doors — Eggys (near, close) + epi thyrais (at the doors) = immediacy idiom for imminent arrival. The subject is ambiguous: the end, or the Son of Man; the ambiguity is probably intentional.
34: ou mē parelthu hē genea autē — this generation will by no means pass away — Ou mē + aorist subjunctive = strongest negation. Genea autē (this generation) is the key exegetical question: Jesus’ contemporaries (first-century fulfillment in AD 70 for the near horizon) or the generation that sees the end-time signs (far-horizon reading). The double referent allows both readings.
35: ho ouranos kai hē gē pareleusontai hoi de logoi mou ou mē parelthōsin — heaven and earth will pass away but my words will by no means pass away — The contrast between cosmic impermanence (pareleusontai = will pass away, future indicative) and the permanence of Jesus’ words (ou mē parelthōsin = will by no means pass away) is a Christological claim: Jesus’ word has greater ontological stability than creation itself.
36: peri de tēs hēmeras ekeinēs kai hōras oudeis oiden — but concerning that day and hour no one knows — Oudeis oiden (no one knows) — absolute ignorance across all categories: oude hoi angeloi tōn ouranōn (not even the angels of heaven) and oude ho huios (not even the Son — present in some MSS, absent in others; Matthean text-critical debate). Ei mē ho patēr monos (except the Father alone).
37: hōsper gar ai hēmerai tou Nōe houtōs estai hē parousia — for as the days of Noah so will be the Parousia — The Noah analogy emphasizes normalcy and surprise: trogontes kai pinontes (eating and drinking), gamoyntes kai gamizonte s (marrying and giving in marriage) — ordinary life, not eschatological alertness. Heōs hēs ēmeras eisēlthen Nōe eis tēn kibōton (until the day Noah entered the ark).
38: ēsan gar en tais hēmerais ekeinais priston tou kataklysmou trogontes kai pinontes — for in those days before the flood they were eating and drinking — Kataklysmos (flood, inundation — from kataklyzo = to flood over) is the standard Greek term for the Noahic flood. Priston tou kataklysm ou (before the flood) — the total unawareness despite Noah’s preaching.
39: kai ouk egnosan hōs ēlthen ho kataklysmos — and they did not know until the flood came — Ouk egnōsan (did not know) — deliberate ignorance or culpable inattention; the flood’s arrival (ēlthen) broke their normalcy without warning. Kai ēren hapantas (and took them all away) — airō (to lift up, take away) in the judgment sense. Houtōs estai kai hē parousia tou huiou tou anthrōpou (so will be the Parousia of the Son of Man).
40: tote duo esontai en tō̄ agrō̧ — then two will be in the field — The paired-person scenarios (field, mill) show that identical external situations will have opposite outcomes. Heis paralam bānetai kai heis aphietai (one is taken and one is left) — paralambanō (to take alongside, to receive) vs. aphiēmi (to leave behind, dismiss) — the direction of the verbs suggests the taken one is received/saved and the left one is abandoned to judgment (though interpretations vary).
41: dyo alēthousai en tō̄ mylōni — two women grinding at the mill — Alēthō (to grind grain) + mylos (mill, millstone) — domestic labor, typically women’s work. The same division: mia paralambanetai kai mia aphietai (one is taken and one is left). The separation occurs within ordinary domestic life, not during extraordinary circumstances.
42: grēgoreite oun hoti ouk oidate poiā hēmera ho kyrios hymōn erchetai — watch therefore because you do not know on what day your Lord comes — Grēgoreō (to be awake, be alert, watch — from egerthēnai = to arise from sleep) is the eschatological watchword. The ignorance of the day (ouk oidate) motivates continuous vigilance, not passive waiting.
43: ekeino de ginōskete hoti ei ē̄dei ho oikodespotēs — but know this: if the householder had known — Oikodespotēs (householder, master of the house) is used in a second-class conditional (ei + aorist indicative... an + aorist indicative) = contrary to fact: he did not know, so he was robbed. Poia phylakē̄ ho klēptēs erchetai (what watch the thief comes) — the night was divided into four watches (Roman system).
44: dia touto kai hymeis ginesthe heti moi hoi — therefore you also be ready — Ginesthe h etoimoi (present imperative: keep being ready) — ongoing state of preparedness, not a one-time act. Hō̄ ou dokei ōra (at an hour you do not expect) — the unexpected hour is the precise one that comes; expectation does not determine timing.
45: tis ara estin ho pistos doulos kai phronimos — who then is the faithful and wise servant? — The parable of the faithful and wicked servants interprets eschatological watchfulness through the figure of a household steward. Pistos kai phronimos (faithful and wise) — the pairing of reliability and practical wisdom. Epi tēs oiketeias autou (over his household) — oiketeia (household, domestic staff) — the steward’s responsibility is the whole household’s provision.
46: makarios ho doulos ekeinos hon elthōn ho kyrios autou heurēsei houtōs poiounta — blessed is that servant whom his lord when he comes will find so doing — Makārios (blessed — the Beatitude word) applied to the faithful servant found at work on the master’s return. Poiounta (present participle: doing, actively engaged) — not waiting passively but actively serving.
47: epi pasin tois hyparchousin autou katastesei auton — over all his possessions he will appoint him — Kath istēmi (to appoint, install, set in place) — the faithful steward’s reward is expanded responsibility, not retirement. Epi pasin tois hyparchousin (over all his possessions) = complete estate management.
48: ean de eipē̄ ho kakos doulos ekeinos — but if that wicked servant says — The third-class conditional (ean + aorist subjunctive) presents the wicked servant’s reasoning as possible, not certain. Chronizei mou ho kyrios (my master is delayed) — chronizō (to take time, delay, be a long time) — the delay itself becomes the rationalization for misconduct.
49: arxētai de typtein tous syndoulous autou — he begins to beat his fellow servants — Syndoūlos (fellow servant, co-slave) — the wicked servant exploits those under him while the master is absent. Esthī̄ de kai pinē̄ meta tōn methyontōn (and eats and drinks with the drunk ones) — self-indulgence and violent domination are paired as the twin abuses of delegated authority.
50: hēxei ho kyrios tou doulou ekeinou en hēmera h̄ ou prosdokā̄ — the master of that servant will come on a day he does not expect — Hēxei (will come, future indicative of hēkō = to have arrived, to be present) — the arrival is certain; only the timing is unknown. The doubled ou prosdokā̄ (does not expect) + en hōrā̄ ou ginōskei (at an hour he does not know) emphasizes complete unpreparedness.
51: dichotomēsei auton kai to meros autou meta tōn hypokritōn thēsei — he will cut him in two and put his portion with the hypocrites — Dichotomēsei (to cut in two, to bisect — from dicha = in two + temnō = to cut) is a brutal image of complete destruction/dismemberment used for execution. Meta tōn hypokritōn (with the hypocrites) — the final category for the unfaithful steward. Ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn recurs as the judgment formula.
